title: Ultra-Confinement of Polaritons in Single Atomic Layer Ag Photonic Quantum Dots
authors: Xinyi Li, Tetyana Ignatova, Chengye Dong, Krishnan Mekkanamkulam Ananthanarayanan, Rinu Abraham Maniyara, Arpit Jain, Furkan Turker, Vinay Kammarchedu, Aida Ebrahimi, Joshua A. Robinson, Slava V. Rotkin
abstract: Light scattering by two-dimensional (2D) van der Waals heterostructures (vdWHs) is immense, especially given their infinitesimal volume, thus enabling strong light-matter interactions. Surface 2D polariton waves manifest through large concentration of electromagnetic field in vertical direction, normal to their propagation. By confining vdWH materials into 2D photonic shapes, one can manipulate and compress light in lateral directions. Scattering-type scanning near-field optical microscopy is a perfect tool for direct imaging of the propagating polaritons and studying the properties of confined polaritons in nanostructures. Though, thus far the quantitative analysis, such the wavelength extraction, has been challenged for confined polaritons by incapability of mapping of the wave period on sub-wavelength scale and difficulty of identifying an adequate substrate's "background" to subtract. Here, an analytical approach is developed to reveal the local propagation constant of confined polaritons under abovementioned constraints and map it with the sub-wavelength resolution. Applied to analysis of the SiC/2D-Ag/EG (epitaxial graphene) photonic nanostructures, the technique uncovered that the polaritons are highly confined in both vertical ($\simλ$/50) and lateral directions ($\simλ$/40) by 2D metal.
summary: The paper introduces a new analytical technique using Argand diagrams to study surface polaritons in nanostructures smaller than the polariton wavelength. It demonstrates that a single atomic layer of silver can ultra-confine electromagnetic fields in both vertical and lateral directions. This method overcomes the limitations of standard near-field microscopy in resolving wave properties in sub-diffraction-sized objects.
main_problem: Quantitatively analyzing and extracting the propagation constant of surface polaritons in sub-diffraction-sized 2D nanostructures where standard sSNOM analysis fails due to lack of interference patterns and background interference.
main_result: Development of an eikonal-based analytical approach using Argand diagrams that successfully reveals the local propagation constant and demonstrates ultra-confinement of polaritons in both vertical (~lambda/50) and lateral (~lambda/40) directions.
method: An eikonal-based representation of the surface polariton wave using Argand plots (real vs. imaginary parts of the sSNOM signal) to trace phase increments and identify wave properties via circular arc fitting.
model_or_system: A 2D van der Waals heterostructure consisting of a single atomic layer of Silver (Ag) encapsulated by epitaxial graphene (EG) on a Silicon Carbide (SiC) substrate, patterned into nano-disks (photonic quantum dots).
key_observables: Third-harmonic demodulated sSNOM signal (amplitude and phase), local propagation constant (k_SP), and confinement factors.
important_parameters: Mid-infrared range (963-1040 cm^-1), nano-disk diameter < 1 um, vertical confinement ~lambda/50, lateral confinement ~lambda/40.
main_assumptions: The photonic dot possesses axial symmetry (radial eikonal wave); the variation in amplitude is slow enough to be ignored when calculating the propagation constant.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Fabrication and atomic-scale morphology (STEM/AFM). Fig 2: sSNOM imaging and raw amplitude. Fig 3: Boundary identification and eikonal wave maps. Fig 4: Dispersion relations. Supplemental: Argand plots, validation against hBN/graphene, and gradient maps.
paper_structure: Introduction of the analytical challenge -> Fabrication and characterization of Ag/EG/SiC nanodisks -> Presentation of the eikonal/Argand analytical method -> Demonstration of confinement and boundary detection -> Validation of the method using different material systems and bulk comparisons.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
